# AI and theological anthropology: what happens to human vocation?

**Series:** Build research / exploratory article  
**Prompt:** [08-ai-theological-anthropology.md](../prompts/08-ai-theological-anthropology.md)  
**Companion raw file:** [../raw/08-ai-theological-anthropology-raw.md](../raw/08-ai-theological-anthropology-raw.md)  
**Date:** 2026-04-12  

---

## 1. Why this layer matters

Most platform companies talk about AI as **capability**: speed, scale, personalization, safety. Movemental’s deeper claim—documented across internal messaging and book trajectory—is that generative AI is primarily an **anthropological** and **vocational** crisis: it rearranges what people trust, how they work, how they form others, and what counts as **presence**. That is not an eccentric reading. It sits near the center of a rapidly growing **Christian and ecumenical** conversation. It also raises a fair challenge: when theology meets product strategy, how do you keep doctrine from becoming **decorative**?

This article maps the wider landscape, situates Movemental inside it, tests the “furnace and hearth” metaphor, and ends with communication discipline for **both** religious and secular audiences.

---

## 2. The landscape: who is speaking, and from which toolkit?

### 2.1 Early Protestant-Lutheran science dialogue

Noreen Herzfeld’s *In Our Image: Artificial Intelligence and the Human Spirit* (Fortress, 2002) remains a touchstone: a **computer scientist** working in **systematic theology**, asking how the drive to build minds in our image refracts the **imago Dei** and rival anthropologies. Brent Waters’s *From Human to Posthuman* (Routledge, 2006) framed technology as a **spiritual–cultural force** shaping late modern hope and fear—again not “about ChatGPT,” but indispensable for **creaturely limits** and resistance to **reductive futurisms**.

### 2.2 Evangelical policy and congregational bridge

Jason Thacker’s *The Age of AI* (Zondervan, 2020) and the Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission’s wider work crystallize a **mainstream evangelical** posture: AI is **not savior**; dignity is grounded in **image-bearing**, not utility; tools require **wisdom** and **transparency**. The Southern Baptist Convention’s **2023** resolution “On Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies” extends that posture into **denominational law**—image-of-God language, warnings about deception and dehumanization, and calls for Christlike use in workplaces and civic life (`https://www.sbc.net/resource-library/resolutions/on-artificial-intelligence-and-emerging-technologies/`). None of this depends on Movemental; it is **shared public goods** Movemental can cite with confidence.

### 2.3 Catholic and ecumenical macro-ethics

The **Rome Call for AI Ethics** (Pontifical Academy for Life, **28 February 2020**) articulates principles—transparency, inclusion, responsibility, impartiality, reliability, security/privacy—that read partly like **secular AI ethics** and partly like **Catholic social teaching** translated for industry signatories (`https://www.romecall.org/`). Subsequent **multi-religious** signings (e.g. Hiroshima “AI Ethics for Peace,” 2024; Anglican participation) show institutional Christianity trying to **shape governance**, not only pastoral guidance.

The World Council of Churches’ **June 2023** statement on **unregulated** generative AI goes further toward **prophetic** register: corporate concentration, digital divide, risks to democracy, autonomous weapons—and explicitly names **idolatry** and the need for **Christian anthropology** in public discourse (`https://www.oikoumene.org/resources/documents/statement-on-the-unregulated-development-of-artificial-intelligence`). That document’s footnotes even reach back to Faith & Order work on **theological anthropology** and AI’s challenge to **human uniqueness**.

### 2.4 Philosophy of technology as shared lingua franca

Albert Borgmann’s **device paradigm**—technologies that deliver **commodities** while hiding **practices**—and James K.A. Smith’s **cultural liturgies**—habits that form us beneath the level of argument—are not “Christian AI books.” They are **standard references** in sermons, seminary classes, and Silicon Valley Christian reading groups. They help explain **why** “AI that writes your prayers” feels different from “AI that spell-checks your newsletter”: not because the model differs, but because the **practice being displaced** differs.

### 2.5 Is there a distinctly *Christian* anthropology of AI?

**Yes and no.** *Yes*: imago Dei, creation–fall–redemption, vocation, Sabbath, sacrament, ecclesiology—these give **non-exchangeable** reasons to limit certain uses. *No*: much “Christian AI ethics” today **imports** EU-style risk language, corporate **ESG** grammar, or utilitarian harm calculus. That is not inherently wrong—**neighbor love** should engage common reason—but it means Movemental must be precise when claiming **theological uniqueness**. What is often unique is not the **policy list** but the **ends of human life** named: **communion with God**, **holiness**, **worship**, **truthful witness**, **forgiveness**—goods secular frameworks under-specify.

### 2.6 Catholic social thought vs. typical Protestant-evangelical emphasis

Catholic engagement often arrives **institutionally** first: principles for **industry** cooperation, human dignity, solidarity, subsidiarity, care for creation—then casuistry on applications (labor, investment, war). Protestant-evangelical engagement (Thacker, SBC) often begins **congregationally**: deception, pornography deepfakes, pastoral integrity, parenting, work—then moves outward to policy. **Overlap** is substantial at the level of “human beings are ends, not means.” **Difference** is emphasis: Catholic documents frequently tie AI to **global governance** and **peace**; evangelical resolutions often tie AI to **family**, **sexual ethics**, and **truth in public witness**. Movemental’s **movement-leader** frame sits somewhat **between** the two: less **parish-programmatic** than typical evangelical bullet lists, less **curial** than Vatican diplomacy—closer to **public theology** with an **organizational** edge.

### 2.7 Ecosystem note: “AI and Faith,” Peters, and religion-and-science journals

Beyond single-author monographs, much contemporary work circulates through **networks**: *Theology and Science*, the **AI and Faith** community, GTU-affiliated scholars, and ad hoc **working groups** inside seminaries. Ted Peters’s public-facing pieces ask whether AI becomes a **spiritual technology**—a way to **simulate virtue** without the slow work of character. That question is **structurally parallel** to Movemental’s **formation** claims even when vocabulary differs. For a platform narrative, the practical takeaway is: **you are not alone** in claiming AI is **soul-shaping**; the task is to **join** that ecumenical conversation with **specificity** (what you will not productize, and why).

---

## 3. Embodiment, presence, and digital ministry

Sherry Turkle’s long arc of research (*Alone Together*, etc.) supplies **sociological color**: what happens when convenience reshapes expectations of attention, vulnerability, and patience. James K.A. Smith supplies **theological structure**: we are **desiring**, **embodied** animals formed by **repetition** in communities of practice. Together they support Movemental’s **C-T01** intuition—**embodied ministry cannot be automated**—if “embodied” is parsed carefully.

The COVID era produced a **burst** of academic and pastoral reflection on “distanced church.” Bibliometric surfaces include titles such as *The Absence of Presence and the Presence of Absence* (2020, widely cited in the sample set)—evidence that the church’s argument was never purely **Luddite**; it was often **sacramental realist**: digital channels can **carry** care, teaching, and even beauty; the contested question is whether they **substitute for** the same **kind** of presence at the font and table. Movemental’s safer public formulation—“**digital supports presence, never replaces it**”—lands inside that mainstream debate, not outside it.

What COVID **did not** settle is the **hybrid equilibrium**: emergency permissions morphed into **permanent expectations** for frictionless consumption of religious content. From a Smithian lens, that is dangerous not because pixels are evil, but because **liturgies of convenience** compete with **liturgies of patience**—and patience is where much **pastoral authority** is actually earned.

Neuroscience’s **4E cognition** stories can be spun two ways: either “we were always hybrid with tools, so relax,” or “**extended mind** means outsourced cognition **re-shapes** skill and character.” For vocational theology, the second use is usually more honest: **habits of attention** are malleable; **delegation** has formation cost. The honest platform stance acknowledges **tradeoffs**: summarization saves time; it also **skims** texts readers might have been **changed** by if they stayed in the friction longer.

---

## 4. “Refusal as love” and sacred red lines

Movemental’s **C-A05** language—“refusal as love,” protecting **sacred** human functions—is **rhetorically striking** but **not** a standard keyword in the theological AI index (at least not yet). Its **nearest respectable cousins** are:

- **Sabbath** non-use as **witness** to creaturely dependence on God—not merely rest for productivity’s sake.  
- **Virtue** questions (Ted Peters and others): does AI offer a **shortcut to holiness**—or replace **struggle** that was doing spiritual work?  
- **Agrarian / Wendell Berry**-style refusal of “inevitability,” where limits protect **neighborliness** and **place**.

Secular AI ethics also names **red lines**—autonomous lethal targeting, certain surveillance modes—but the grounding is usually **harm**, not **sanctity**. The difference matters: **sacred refusal** can prohibit an action **even when** risk is low, because the act **mis-shapes souls** or **liturgizes** false worship. Movemental should expect **skepticism** here from both engineers (“define measurable harm”) and **liberal Protestants** (“is this legalism?”). The answer is **thick description**: name **which** practices are protected (e.g. pastoral presence at deathbeds; confession; spiritual direction; public repentance), **why** automation would deform them, and **where** AI clearly **serves** without pretending to be those things.

### 4.1 Interfaith resonances (without overclaiming)

Jewish legal reasoning around **Shabbat** and instruments offers a mature model of **time-bounded** refusal—not anti-technology in general, but **anti-sovereignty** of technology over covenant rhythm. Islamic traditions contain long-standing **image-making** cautions and fresh **fiqh** debates on generative media; Buddhist communities often foreground **attention** and **non-deception**—both highly relevant to chatbot pastoralia. Movemental should treat these as **dialogue invitations** and **analogical resources**, not as tags proving “we read everyone.” Comparative work needs **specialists**; public copy needs **humility**.

---

## 5. The “furnace and hearth” metaphor

Internal copy positions AI as a **furnace** that “needs a **hearth**”—high throughput, industrial heat, domestic centering (`docs/articles/01_copy-deck-why-movemental.md`). Genealogically, this looks like a **synthesis**: Borgmann’s **black-box furnace in the basement** meets the **hearth-as-focus** (literally *focus* in Latin) of pre-industrial domestic life and the **biblical furnace** as trial/refinement. The **exact tagline** appears **Movemental-native** in repo-surfaced materials; the **imagery** is not.

**Preaching caution:** some hearers will hear **judgment** (fiery furnace) rather than **productive power**. Testing the line with Catholic, Black church, and Pentecostal readers is wise—**heat metaphors** carry unequal histories.

---

## 6. Where Movemental sits: mainstream, contrarian, or innovative?

**Mainstream:** Anthropological framing; imago Dei dignity; skepticism of utopianism; hybrid ministry cautions; regulatory concern—Movemental is **aligned** with large swaths of SBC, WCC, Catholic-industry dialogues, and Herzfeld’s older questions.

**Contrarian (relative to tech culture):** refusal language, **anti-solo-empire** communitas, and explicit **Christocentric** load-bearing for a **platform** are **countercultural** in product marketing—even if **normal** in ecclesial ethics.

**Innovative (modest claim):** not a new **doctrine of God**, but a **strategic fusion**: vocational theology + **adaptive leadership** (Heifetz) + **credibility / network verification** (sibling research briefs)—aimed at **movement leaders** rather than seminary faculties alone. That **audience targeting** is the real differentiator.

---

## 7. The hardest question: theology or branding?

Secular readers will ask whether “Jesus at the center” is **integrity** or **segmentation**. The integrity move is simple to describe and hard to execute: **public ethics** that track **private governance** (who trains models on whose words; what is sold; what is refused), **named theologians** in footnotes where claims are heavy, and **humility** about what software can do for souls.

Academic theologians will ask for **traditioned argument**: not only *that* formation matters, but *how*—Augustine on habit, Luther on vocation, Reformed catechisms on law and gospel, Catholic CST on **subsidiarity** and **participation**, liberation and womanist critiques of **whose** embodiment counted first. Movemental does not need to become a seminary press, but it **does** need **citation discipline** if it wants **intellectual** authority to match its **rhetorical** authority.

---

## 8. Speaking to religious and secular audiences without splitting the self

**For secular partners and press:** translate **sacred refusal** into **professional ethics** (“we do not automate pastoral care claims”), **transparency**, **consent**, **provenance**, **human accountability**—then **add** that there are **non-instrumental** reasons beneath those rules. Secular audiences often respect **integrity** more than **jargon**.

**For church audiences:** show **continuity** with Rome Call / WCC / SBC where possible—this is **catholicity** (small-c), not party politics. Reserve **distinctive** language (furnace/hearth, communitas) for moments when it **does explanatory work**.

**For both:** avoid implying AI is **intrinsically** demonic or salvific; **Thacker’s** “tool requiring wisdom” is a **shared baseline** that still permits strong limits.

### 8.1 Concrete “translation table” (examples only)

| Theological claim (internal) | Secular / ecumenical translation | What would contradict integrity |
|------------------------------|----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Christ is load-bearing, not decorative | “We do not optimize revenue by simulating spiritual authority.” | AI personas mimicking named leaders without disclosure |
| Refusal protects the sacred | “Some outputs are out of scope; here is the policy.” | “Anything for engagement” exceptions |
| Embodiment cannot be automated | “Accountability requires traceable human responsibility.” | Unattributed bulk generation presented as personal witness |
| Communitas / shared risk | “Credibility is networked, not solo.” | Leaderboard dynamics that pit members against each other |

The table is not magic; it is **editorial discipline**. Theology becomes **branding** when the translations are **advertised** but the contradictions are **tolerated**.

### 8.2 Denominational gaps worth filling later

This research pass located **strong** artifacts for SBC, WCC, Rome Call, Church of England, and PC(USA). **Methodist** and **global Anglican** instruments beyond England deserve a dedicated sweep; PCA appears more **diffuse** (commentary without a single resolution). A living **inventory doc** maintained by staff would prevent outdated “first denomination ever” claims as more bodies publish.

---

## 9. Closing

Theological anthropology is doing **real work** in Movemental’s argument stack: it explains **why** credibility, embodiment, and refusal belong in one story. Much of that work is **not proprietary**—it rests on Herzfeld, Waters, Smith, Borgmann, Catholic and ecumenical statements, and a wave of post-2020 literature. Movemental’s opportunity is **synthesis and integrity**: hold the **Christocentric** center as **load-bearing**, tether metaphors to **tradition**, and let **product decisions** prove whether the theology was **operational**—not merely **ornamental**.

---

- Herzfeld, N. *In Our Image: Artificial Intelligence and the Human Spirit* (Fortress, 2002).  
- Waters, B. *From Human to Posthuman* (Routledge, 2006).  
- Thacker, J. *The Age of AI* (Zondervan, 2020).  
- Southern Baptist Convention. “On Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies” (2023). https://www.sbc.net/resource-library/resolutions/on-artificial-intelligence-and-emerging-technologies/  
- Rome Call for AI Ethics (2020). https://www.romecall.org/  
- World Council of Churches. “Statement on the Unregulated Development of Artificial Intelligence” (2023). https://www.oikoumene.org/resources/documents/statement-on-the-unregulated-development-of-artificial-intelligence

- Borgmann, A. *Technology and the Character of Contemporary Life* (University of Chicago Press, 1984).
